title: Suicide Prevention Assistance Act
plain_summary: This bill creates a new program that gives money to primary care doctors to help prevent suicide. The doctors must use the money to hire social workers who will check patients to see if they might hurt themselves. If someone is at risk, the social workers will provide help right away and connect them with other services they might need later. The goal is to stop people from killing themselves by catching the warning signs early when they visit their regular doctor.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Energy and Commerce.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Suicide Prevention Assistance Act.

sections.title: Section 2 Grants to Provide Self-Harm and Suicide Prevention Services
sections.text:
The Secretary of Health and Human Services will give money grants to primary care doctor offices (places where people go for regular medical care) to help provide services that prevent self-harm and suicide. The Assistant Secretary for Mental Health and Substance Use will run this program.

Doctor offices that get grants must use the money for these specific things. First, they must hire one or more clinical social workers (trained professionals who help people with mental health and social problems). Second, the primary care doctors must check patients to see if they might hurt themselves or think about suicide, following official guidelines. When a doctor finds warning signs, they must tell the social worker. Third, the social worker must give patients short-term help to prevent self-harm and suicide based on what the screening showed. Fourth, when needed, the social worker must send patients to other healthcare places for long-term help with preventing self-harm and suicide.

The government can only give out ten grants total. Each doctor office can only get one grant. Only one doctor office in each state can get a grant.

Each grant cannot be more than five hundred thousand dollars. The grant lasts for two years. Doctor offices can apply to renew their grants if they still meet the requirements.

Doctor offices that want a grant must send in an application to the Secretary. The Secretary will decide what information needs to be included and when applications are due.

Within one hundred eighty days after this law starts, the Secretary must create official guidelines for how doctors should check patients for self-harm and suicide risks. The Secretary must work with expert groups that know about preventing self-harm and suicide when creating these guidelines. These expert groups include public agencies, private companies, and nonprofit organizations.

Doctor offices that get grants must send reports to the Secretary at least every three months during their grant period. These reports must evaluate how well the grant activities are working. The reports must include how many patients got screenings at the doctor office, how many got short-term prevention services at the office, and how many were sent to other healthcare places for long-term help. The reports must also show whether the doctor office is following the official screening guidelines and include other information the Secretary thinks is important for judging how grant money is being used.

Two years after this law starts, and then every two years after that, the Secretary must send a report about this grant program to specific congressional committees and to parts of the Department of Health and Human Services. The report must summarize all the reports received from doctor offices and include the Secretary's evaluation of how well the program is working. The parts of the Department of Health and Human Services that get these reports are the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention and the National Institute of Mental Health.

This section defines important terms. Appropriate congressional committees means the Committee on Energy and Commerce in the House of Representatives and the Committee on Health, Education, Labor, and Pensions in the Senate. Primary care office means a healthcare place that provides primary care services (basic medical care). State means any of the fifty states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico, or any other territory or area controlled by the United States.
